- Calling costs 1000, leaving me with 2800 chips
- This crippled stack would make me extremely vulnerable on the river
- If I miss (76% of the time), I'm left in a precarious position with minimal flexibility

Risk assessment:
- Elon has shown consistent aggression (button raiser, flop bet 400, turn bet 1000)
- If I call and miss the river, he'll likely bet again, leaving me in an untenable spot
- If I call and hit, I win a decent pot, but the risk of the 76% whiff is too costly given stack depth
- The marginal equity doesn't justify crippling my stack further

Against a shorter-stacked or different opponent, this marginal equity with pot odds might justify a call. However, given that Elon's aggressive style will likely result in another river bet if I miss, and my shallow remaining stack, folding preserves better positioning for upcoming hands.

This is a disciplined fold on a marginal situation.

UTG position with A3o (offsuit Ace-Three). This is a very weak hand from early position in a 4-handed game. Standard UTG ranges do not include A3o; it's dominated by better aces, has poor playability post-flop, and doesn't justify risking chips pre-flop when deep-stacked (123BB). Opponents are aggressive but folding preserves stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

I am on the button with AQo, which is a very strong starting hand in a 4-handed game. Elon has already folded UTG. Standard strategy here is to open for a raise to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is consistent with the table's betting patterns and puts pressure on the remaining players while keeping me in control of the hand with positional advantage.

8dQc is a weak hand that doesn't merit playing from the Small Blind out of position. Flash's button raise from 300 has a wide range, and I'd be consistently outplayed post-flop in position. My stack of 3750 is healthy enough that I don't need to gamble with marginal holdings. I should preserve chips for premium hands where I can play with positional advantage. Clear fold.

Heads-up in the big blind with Q♠T♥ facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and it costs me 200 more (300 total committed) to see the flop. QTo is a good defending hand vs a wide BTN open: playable postflop, decent equity, and gives me options without overcommitting out of position. A min 3-bet to 500 would bloat the pot OOP with limited fold equity and little extra value from this hand. I’ll call to keep the pot controlled, realize equity, and evaluate on the flop.

Check. On 3♦ 9♦ 9♥ with Q♠T♥ I have two overcards and about ~24% to pair by the river but no backdoor flush and very limited fold equity OOP. Flash’s BTN open range includes a lot of hands that connect with this board (9x, pocket pairs, overpairs), so a flop c-bet will often get called and bloat a pot I’m not inclined to be committed to with just overcards. Checking controls the pot (650) and lets me realize equity cheaply: I’ll call small continuation bets to see a turn and fold to large pressure, and if I hit a Q or T on the turn I can fire for value or extract folds. Given stacks (I have 10,500) this is the prudent, value-preserving line.

On a paired board like 3d-9d-9h, I have a strong range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. Although I missed the flop with AQo, I have two overcards and the Ace of diamonds for a backdoor flush draw. Minni's check is expected. A standard continuation bet of about half the pot (300) should be enough to fold out her weaker hands that missed this flop, such as small suited connectors or broadway cards that didn't connect. If I am called, I still have equity and position to navigate the turn.

Flash c-bet 300 into ~650 (pot now 950). I hold Q♠T♥ on 3♦9♦9♥ — two clean overcards with 6 outs to pair (3 Qs + 3 Ts). Calling 300 gets me a pot of 1250, so break-even equity is 300/1250 ≈ 24%. My chance to hit by the river is ≈24% so this is essentially a pot-odds-driven, low-cost call. Raising OOP with no pair has poor fold equity and bloats a pot I don’t want out of position; folding would be too tight given the direct pot odds and Flash’s wide BTN range. I’ll call to realize equity, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate on the turn — fold to large turn aggression if I miss, or pursue value (bet/caller) if I pair Q or T.

I just paired my Queen on the turn (Q♠T♥ on 3♦9♦9♥Q♦). I’ve got top pair with a reasonable (but not great) kicker against Flash’s wide BTN opening/continuation range. He c‑bet the flop and I called, so his continuing range contains many worse Qx, Kx/Ax, some Jx and weaker broadways — plus the occasional strong made hand (sets/quads/QQ/Q9 combos). Betting here extracts value from the worse Qx and other hands that will call a modest bet, denies a free river card to some of his equity, and keeps the pot controlled against the portion of his range that crushes me.

I’ll put out a medium value bet (≈40% pot) — 500 chips into the 1,250 pot. That size is large enough to get value from worse Queens and many calling hands, but small enough to avoid bloating the pot unnecessarily vs sets/full houses. If Flash raises big, I’ll assume a very strong holding (full house/quads/strong double‑diamond flush) and release; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and extract further only on safe runouts or check/call when appropriate.

On this turn, the Queen of diamonds is an excellent card for my hand. I have improved to top pair (Queens) with the nut flush draw (Ace of diamonds). Minni's lead of 500 into a 1250 pot (a 'donk' bet) is interesting. She could be leading with a nine, a made flush, or a weaker Queen. Since I hold the Ace of diamonds, I know she doesn't have the nut flush. I have significant equity against her entire range; even if she has trips or a lower flush, I have nine outs to the nuts. Calling is the best play here to keep her bluffs in, control the size of the pot, and utilize my positional advantage on the river.

Check. The Ace on the river (Ac) bricks a “safe” runout and significantly improves many hands in Flash’s turn-calling range (any Ax, trips/sets with 99/33/A9, some two-diamond flushes, and better Qx like KQ/AQ). My Q♠T♥ is a marginal top pair with a medium kicker — it has limited showdown value here and little fold equity. A river value bet would mainly fold out the weak Qx hands I beat and get called (or raised) by the hands that already beat me, so it’s a negative risk/reward. Checking controls the pot (2250), lets missed diamond draws bluff, and allows me to call a reasonable river bluff while folding to polarizing raises. I’ll check to see what Flash does and then call small bluffs but fold to large river aggression.

The river Ace is a very strong card for my hand, giving me top two pair (Aces and Nines). Minni's check on the river after leading out on the turn is telling. It suggests she may have been betting a Queen or a draw and is now concerned about the Ace, which hits my pre-flop raising range perfectly. While I could potentially value bet here against an Ace with a weaker kicker or a Queen, the board is coordinated enough (possible flushes and trips) that checking back is the safest and most reliable way to realize my equity and win the pot. Checking ensures I win against her bluffs and marginal hands without exposing myself to a potential check-raise from a monster hand like a nine or a flush.

I am UTG with a premium pocket pair (Jacks). This is a strong opening hand that should be played aggressively to thin the field and build value. A standard raise size of 3x the big blind (300) is consistent with my previous play and the current table dynamic.

I'm on the button with As8h, facing Flash's raise to 300 from UTG. This is a decent ace with decent kicker. Analysis: Flash has been very aggressive throughout the session (I've seen him raise preflop frequently), so his range here is quite wide. I have position, which is valuable, and reasonable pot odds (1.5:1 with 450 in the pot). The key advantages of calling are: (1) I'm in position for the rest of the hand, (2) I keep two other players (Minni in SB and Elon in BB) in the decision, which increases the chance they contribute chips to the pot, and (3) my hand plays reasonably well in multiway pots and especially well heads-up. My stack is the shortest at the table (3750), so I should be thoughtful about investing, but this is a borderline hand in position with good pot odds and the ability to see what develops before committing further. I'll call and take advantage of my positional advantage.

I have pocket Jacks (Js Jc) in the small blind facing an UTG open to 300 with the button calling. Multiway OOP with JJ is suboptimal — I want to isolate the opener and take the initiative. I’ll reraise to 1000 total (a standard ~3x+ sizing vs a 300 open with one caller) to gain fold equity, charge speculative hands, and discourage loose flats. If I face a very large 4-bet (~2.5k–3k+) I’ll strongly consider folding given my positional disadvantage; if called I’ll c-bet on dry, low-card flops and proceed cautiously on Ace/King-high or coordinated boards. Total committed preflop will be 1000 (including my 50 SB).

T7o is a speculative offsuit hand with poor playability and equity multiway. Facing a UTG raise from Flash, a flat call from Claude on the button, and a 3-bet to 1000 from Minni in the SB, this is a clear fold. The pot is 1700, calling 900 commits too much with trash that doesn't play well postflop against a strong range. My stack is 12200, no need to speculate here. History shows aggressive play, but position and hand strength dictate folding.

I have pocket Jacks in early position and opened with a standard raise. Minni has 3-bet from the small blind after Claude called my initial raise. Pocket Jacks are a premium hand, but Minni's 3-bet from out of position into two players suggests a very strong range (typically JJ+, AQs+, AKo). While a 4-bet is an option to exert pressure, calling is the more prudent play here to keep the pot manageable and see a flop. I will have position on Minni post-flop, although Claude (if he calls) will have position on me. Calling allows me to evaluate the board and potentially set-mine or play for value if no overcards appear.

Folding As8o in this 3-bet pot from the SB. Minni's 3-bet indicates a strong range (QQ+, AK, AQ), and Flash's call demonstrates commitment. My hand is weak and out-of-position, likely behind both opponents. With a 3.4:1 pot odds requirement needing ~23% equity, and my hand likely at that threshold at best against their ranges, this is a preservation spot with my short stack (3450). Better spots will emerge where I can accumulate chips more favorably.

I 3‑bet preflop to take the initiative and am now in position on a very dry, rainbow 7h-3s-9d flop with an overpair (JJ). This is an ideal board to continuation-bet: many of Flash’s UTG calling hands (missed broadways, single overcards, some suited connectors) will fold, while worse pairs (7x, 3x) and draws will either call or pay to try to improve. I’ll make a value/pressure c-bet sized to ~2/3 pot (1600) to extract value from worse pairs, charge straight draws (e.g. 8-10 combos) and deny free cards. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously vs heavy aggression (folding to large, polarized raises that represent sets or two-pair), but continue to target thin value and fold equity while in position.

I have pocket Jacks on a very favorable flop (7h 3s 9d) with no overcards. Pre-flop, Minni 3-bet from the small blind, which indicates a strong range including AA, KK, QQ, JJ, TT, and AK. Her 1600 bet into the 2400 pot on the flop is a standard continuation bet. While I am ahead of hands like TT, AK, and AQ, I am behind AA, KK, and QQ. Raising now might fold out her bluffs (AK, AQ) while getting called or shoved on by the hands that beat me. By calling, I keep her range wider, maintain position, and can see how she reacts on the turn. My hand is too strong to fold but calling is more strategic than raising to manage the pot size and extract value from bluffs.

I have an overpair (JJ) on a very dry board (7h-3s-9d-6s) and I’m in position. The 6s doesn’t change my relative hand strength much other than giving a small chance that Flash has turned a set (66) — but his calling line on the flop is also consistent with one-pair hands (99/77/3x), medium pairs, and some straight draws (e.g. 10-8, 8-6). I want to extract value from worse pairs and charge the turn cards that pick up equity while denying free cards, so I’ll continue for value and protection with a half-pot bet. Betting ~2800 makes calls by single pairs and draws incorrect and keeps control of the hand; if Flash check‑raises large (a polarized raise that commits most of my stack) I’ll strongly consider folding to a shove, but I’ll call a single-call to evaluate the river.

I hold pocket Jacks on a board of 7h-3s-9d-6s. This is an overpair to the board. Minni, who 3-bet pre-flop, has continued her aggression with a large flop bet and a half-pot turn bet. Her range includes premium pairs (AA, KK, QQ, JJ, TT) and high-card hands like AK or AQ. While I am behind AA, KK, and QQ, I am ahead of TT and all her bluffing combinations (AK, AQ). Given the pot odds (calling 2,800 to win 8,400, or 3:1), I only need to be right 25% of the time to make this a profitable call. Folding an overpair in a 3-bet pot on this board would be too tight and exploitable, especially against an opponent who has shown high aggression in previous hands. Raising would likely only force out her bluffs and get called by hands that beat me, so calling is the best play to maintain position and potentially induce further bluffs on the river.

I hold JJ on 7h-3s-9d-6s-Ac. I 3‑bet preflop and barreled flop and turn; Flash (UTG) has passively called every street. That line weights his continuing range to value (pocket pairs, some made straights from 10-8, and A‑x combos that now beat me on the river when an Ace shows). A river bet here will often fold out the weak one-pair hands I beat (who are now scared by the Ace) and will be called or raised by the hands that actually beat me (Ax, straights, sets). Flash’s passive tendencies make river bluffs unlikely, so betting for thin value is low EV and exposes me to a check‑raise I don't want to call with a short remaining stack. With pot ≫ my stack and little chance to extract profitable calls, I check and go to showdown rather than risking more chips.

The river Ace is a significant scare card. Minni's 3-betting range pre-flop and subsequent barreling on the flop and turn suggest she holds a premium pair (AA, KK, QQ, JJ, TT) or big cards like AK. While the Ace on the river is a great card for her AK hands, her check could indicate she is either trap-checking with a hit or that she's given up/playing cautiously with hands like KK or QQ. Given the size of the pot and the fact that I have showdown value with pocket Jacks but am unlikely to be called by any worse hands if I bet, checking back is the most sensible play to minimize further risk and see the showdown.

With 5-7 hearts in UTG (under the gun) position, I should fold. While the hand is suited, it's a weak holding that plays poorly out of position. From the previous hands, I can see aggressive opponents (Minni, Elon, Flash) who are active in the game. My stack of 3450 is moderate, and I should preserve chips for stronger hands. UTG is the worst position to play marginal hands. Folding is the mathematically correct decision here.

I’m on the button with T4o. Hand is marginal but position is strong and only the blinds remain — a small 2x open (to 200) is a standard steal attempt that gives me fold equity and the initiative. If I’m 3-bet large I’ll fold (T4o has poor equity vs a 3-bet). If called I’ll play postflop in position: c-bet light on dry, uncoordinated flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated/face-up Ace/King/Queen boards. The raise is small relative to effective stacks and preserves optionality while applying pressure to the blinds.

KJ offsuit is a premium hand from the small blind facing a button raise after an UTG fold. Minni's button range is likely wide due to position and prior aggression observed in hand history. KJ blocks strong hands like KQs, AK and has excellent equity (often >45%) against a typical button opening range including suited connectors, broadway, and Ax. 3-betting provides fold equity preflop, isolates against the button, and builds the pot with a strong hand. Standard sizing to 600 (3x the open). While OOP postflop, the hand plays well and stack depths (12k eff with Minni) support it. BB may fold or call light, but we have position advantage over button. Superior to flatting due to initiative and avoiding multiway pots OOP.

I am in the Big Blind with Kd 8s. Minni opened from the Button to 200, and Elon 3-bet from the Small Blind to 600. Kd 8s is a weak hand that does not play well against a 3-betting range, especially from the Small Blind. Calling 500 more out of position against a strong range is a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I opened to 200 on the button with T4o and Elon 3‑betted to 600 (pot = 900), leaving me 400 to call. Calling 400 into 900 requires ~30.8% equity (400/(900+400)). T4o is frequently dominated by a typical SB 3‑bet range (Axs, broadway hands, many Tx/Kx combos) and won’t have the required equity or reliable post‑flop playability even with position. A 4‑bet bluff here is a poor candidate with T4o. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

UTG with 3♠7♦ — one of the weakest starting hands. Facing a preflop raise (need 100 to call to 200) and out of position against a raiser’s fairly strong range, this hand has almost no playable equity or post‑flop plan (offsuit, no straight/flush potential of note). Calling or 3‑betting would be speculative and invite difficult decisions out of position; better to preserve chips for better spots or playable holdings. Fold and wait for a hand with defensive value or positional advantage.

On the button with A9o after UTG fold, this is a standard raising hand due to excellent position, fold equity against blinds, and the hand's playability with an ace blocker. Claude in BB is shorter at ~33BB and may defend wide, but we have initiative. Raise to 300 (3x BB) to build pot with range advantage while keeping it standard sizing seen in prior hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 10s4h. Elon has raised to 300 from the button. 10-4 offsuit is a very weak hand, and playing out of position against a button raiser with such a hand is generally unprofitable. I should fold and wait for a better spot.

With 8h Td in the Big Blind facing Elon's button raise to 300, I have solid pot odds (2.25:1) and reasonable playability. The hand can make straights, has flush draw potential, and performs decently against a wide button range. I'll call 200 more to see the flop with a plan to evaluate the texture and stack-off considerations post-flop.

Checking on the flop with 8h Td on 9d Ad 4c is optimal here. I have approximately 12 clean outs for an OESD (6, 7, J give me multiple straight ways), providing ~36% turn equity. Checking allows me to induce a bet from Elon's button raise while keeping the pot small and controllable with my relatively short 3150-chip stack. If Elon checks behind, I get to see the turn for free with strong equity. If he bets, I can evaluate pot odds and stack-off considerations. My lack of a made hand makes checking far superior to betting here.

Top two pair (aces and nines) on a draw-heavy diamond flush draw board. Claude checked first from OOP after calling my button raise preflop, which suggests weakness or a trap, but my hand is well ahead of his likely calling range (Ax weaker, pairs, draws). Pot is 650, betting ~2/3 pot (450) for value against Ax/draws and protection against diamond flush draws or straight draws (like 5-6, 2-3, 5-7). This sizing builds the pot while denying equity to floats. History shows Claude folds to aggression often (e.g., Hand 9, 19), so good fold equity too. Avoid overbetting vs shorter stack.

With 8h Td against Elon's flop bet on a 9d Ad 4c board, I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs to make a straight with any K, J, or 6) plus two overcards. My current pot odds are approximately 3.44:1 (1550 to call 450), which is favorable for a drawing hand with roughly 32-35% equity.

Given my short stack situation (3150 chips remaining), I need to be strategic about stack-to-pot ratio. While a semi-bluff raise could apply pressure, it would commit me to too much of my remaining stack if Elon re-raises, forcing me into a marginal all-in decision.

Calling 450 is mathematically sound here. If I hit my straight on the turn, I'm likely to win a significant pot. If the turn is a blank, I'll have approximately 2700 chips left and can evaluate the turn action based on the new card and Elon's behavior.

This call preserves my ability to make turns-dependent decisions while maintaining favorable pot odds on a strong draw.

With 16 straight outs after hitting the Jack on the turn (6,7,K,Q all complete different straits), I have approximately 35% equity against most holdings. Elon's check indicates weakness. I'm betting 600 chips (40% of pot) as a semi-bluff that wins the pot immediately if Elon folds, or gets called by weaker hands while I maintain strong equity to hit my draw on the river. This sizing applies pressure without overcommitting my short stack and capitalizes on my improved equity from the turn card.

Top two pair (A▓ 9♠) on a draw-heavy board (diamond flush draw completed possible, spade flush draw opened, straight draws like 10x). Facing BB's check-call flop then turn lead after my initiative. This line from OOP shorter stack (~27BB pre-turn bet) suggests possible Jack improvement (AJ, KJ, QJ for two pair or better kicker), weaker Ax turning into value lead, or semi-bluff/draw. However, my hand crushes most of his range: weaker Ax (A4, A5 etc.), 9x, 4x, draws (KdQd, spade draws), one pair. AJ possible but less likely to check-call flop softly (TPTK strong). Sets/flush less common. Raising for thin value, protection vs draws (board getting scarier), and fold equity vs bluffs/marginal hands. Pot 2150, to-call 600; raise to 2000 (~3.3x his bet, pot-committing him somewhat with 2100 behind) builds pot while maintaining initiative. If he jams AJ/sets, evaluate equity (~35% vs AJ), but range favors us long-term. Folding/checking too passive with nuts-ish hand in position.

With 8h Td and 16 straight outs (6, 7, Q, K), I have ~35% equity against Elon's range. Calling 1400 into a 5700 pot gives me 4.07:1 odds, requiring only ~19.5% equity. This is a profitable call mathematically. My stack is already heavily committed (700 chips remaining post-call), and the equity advantage justifies going with the draw. The Jack on the turn significantly improved my chances.

With 8h Td on a 9d Ad 4c Js 3d river, I have ace-high with no made hand - I missed my straight draws (needed 7 or Q). My hand has marginal but real equity against Elon's mixed range (roughly 30-40%), particularly against missed draws and weaker holdings. 

Checking is strategically superior to betting my remaining 700 chips because: (1) If Elon checks behind, I win the pot with ace-high; (2) If Elon bets, I obtain ~8:1 pot odds, requiring only ~11% equity to call profitably - well below my actual equity; (3) Betting provides no additional information and commits my stack without leverage. 

Checking maintains an honest line (representing a missed draw) while keeping maximum flexibility to win the showdown or make a profitable call based on Elon's river action.

On the river, the board is 9d Ad 4c Js 3d, giving me top two pair with Ah9s (aces and nines). Claude checked after check-calling the flop softly (likely as Ax, pairs, or draws), then leading the turn Js for 600 into ~2150 pot, which I raised to 2000 (pot-committing sizing), and he called 1400 leaving ~700. This aggressive OOP line on turn suggests he improved to a Jack (AJ for superior aces-jacks two pair, KJ/QJ/TJ for jacks + ace or straight draws), or strong Ax value, or turned draw/set. River 3d completes diamond flushes (e.g., KdQd, any diamond Ax without Ad). His check OOP on river after such strength could be pot control with a monster (AJ, flush, set) or trapping showdown value. Weaker hands (A-high no J, missed spade/diamond draws) unlikely to lead/call huge turn OOP short. Betting ~half-pot (2800) risks villain calling/jamming only better hands, as thin value vs overpairs/marginal Jx is poor EV given history of Claude folding to aggression but showing strength here (e.g., Hand 13 river bet). Pot 5550 vs his 700 (~13% pot) is tiny; checking behind realizes equity safely with strong showdown value, denies villain getting 3:1 with bluffs, and preserves stack for future spots. Superior play over spew-y hero calls. Long-term stack maximization favors check.

Pocket threes is a premium hand in a 4-handed game, especially from UTG. It plays well multi-way if called, with strong set-mining potential and implied odds given deep stacks (mine and opponents'). Standard play is to raise to isolate and build the pot. Raising to 3x the BB (300) is balanced and fits my aggressive image from previous hands. Folding is too tight; limping invites multi-way pots without initiative.

I am on the button with 8c 7s. Elon (UTG) has opened to 300. While I have position, 87o is a weak hand to defend against a UTG raise. Furthermore, Claude in the Small Blind is extremely short-stacked with only 6.5 BBs (650 chips), making it highly likely he will move all-in or fold, which complicates the pot. Given the strength of a typical UTG opening range and the marginal nature of my hand, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Short-stacked with 650 chips (M ≈ 4.3) holding K♣T♣ from Small Blind. Elon raised to 300 from UTG with a large stack. Rather than call and play 400 chips short-stacked in a multiway pot, going all-in is more efficient. K♣T♣ is a premium hand for my range in this situation with decent equity against Elon's likely range. This move maximizes fold equity, applies pressure, and represents good value for a broadway hand. Pushing all 650 is optimal compared to calling and playing short-stacked.

Facing an UTG open to 300 and a 3-bet to 650, I'm in the big blind with 4♦2♠ — a very weak, poorly connected, dominated hand. To call 550 into the 1,050 pot would make the pot 1,600, so I’d need ~34.4% equity to justify a call. Versus an UTG opening range and a 3-bettor’s range my actual equity with 4-2o is far below that and the hand has terrible postflop playability out of position. A 4-bet bluff here would be reckless, and there’s no reason to invest chips with such a weak holding. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot.

Pocket 33 is a strong set-mining hand in a deep-stacked 4-handed game. I opened UTG to 300, standard. Claude's SB 3-bet to 650 is aggressive but typical from blinds with a wide range including Ax, suited connectors, broadways. Calling the additional 350 into a pot of 1400 offers ~4:1 direct odds, plus massive implied odds if I hit a set (deep stacks ~10k+ effective). Folding is too tight preflop for a premium small pair. 4-betting is unnecessary and turns it into a bluff when it plays better postflop. OOP is acceptable for set mining. Plan to play aggressively if I flop a set.

I'm critically short-stacked with only 50 chips remaining at the flop, representing less than half a big blind. My hand K♣T♣ on Jd7h4s is K-high with two overcards (K, T, 9), giving me some equity but no made hand currently. Since I'm likely to be all-in very soon regardless, the optimal play is to check and take a free card rather than bloat the pot. This allows me to: (1) potentially see another card for free, (2) let Elon make the betting decisions as the larger stack, and (3) preserve the option to get more favorable odds if he bets. With such minimal chips, I need to be strategic about pot odds rather than aggressive. Checking gives me the most information at the lowest cost.

Pot is 1400 with Claude having only 50 chips left post his preflop 3-bet and flop check. Pocket 3s give a clean pair below a relatively dry rainbow flop (Jd 7h 4s). Claude's SB 3-bet range after my UTG raise includes polarized strong hands (AJ+, 88+) and bluffs (suited connectors, Ax suited). His check suggests weakness or a trap, but more likely missed value since he didn't lead. As original raiser, this is a standard continuation spot OOP. Betting 50 all-in on him wins the pot outright vs folds (common for missed draws/bluffs) or gets called by dominated hands worse than a pair. Checking risks him shoving any two cards or checking down, denying me value from worse. Deep effective stacks otherwise, but exploiting shorty here maximizes EV and builds image.

I'm critically short-stacked with exactly 50 chips remaining - my final chips in this hand. Elon has bet 50 on the flop after I checked. The pot is currently 1450, and I'm being offered 1500:50 or 30:1 odds to call.

My hand K♣T♣ on Jd 7h 4s gives me:
- K-high currently with two overcards
- Potential to make pairs with K or T
- Potential to make a straight with any 9 or 8
- Roughly 30-35% equity in this spot

This is an immediate all-in situation regardless - I either call my final 50 chips or fold. Given the pot odds (30:1) are excellent and my hand has meaningful equity, folding would be surrendering with reasonable equity still available. The mathematical expectation clearly favors calling with these odds.

Calling maximizes my chances to win this pot and stay alive in the tournament.

I’m on the button with Kc6h — a marginal but playable hand in position. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call, so I’ll try to take the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds rather than limping or folding. A raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) is a standard button open size: it gives good fold equity, often isolates to one opponent, and keeps the pot manageable with a hand that fares better in position than out of it. Plan: fold to a meaningful 3-bet (preserve stack against strong ranges), but if called I’ll C‑bet on dry, favorable flops and proceed cautiously (check/call or concede) on coordinated/wet boards. This balances stealing frequency with clear stop-loss lines against heavy aggression.

2s8d is one of the worst possible starting hands: offsuit, unconnected, low cards with no straight or flush potential. Facing a button raise from Minni, who has shown aggression in previous hands (frequent raises preflop), the pot odds to call 250 into 450 (~2.8:1) are insufficient given my hand's equity against even a wide button opening range (dominated by better hands). Folding preserves my deep stack (160x BB) for premium spots. No value in raising or calling.

I am in the big blind holding 3c 4c, a low suited connector. Minni has opened from the button for 300. With the small blind folding, I am closing the action and only need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650. This gives me pot odds of 3.25 to 1. Given the playability of suited connectors and the fact that I am closing the action, I will call to see a flop and hope to hit a strong draw or two pair.

On a board of Js Ad Jd, my 3c 4c has virtually no equity. I have no pair and no real draw. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), the standard play is to check and see how they respond. I will most likely fold to any significant bet as this board hits a Button's opening range very well.

Check. I missed this flop completely (Kc6h) and the board (Js Ad Jd) heavily favors the caller’s range — lots of Ax, Jx and pocket pairs that beat me or call any small bluff. There’s also no meaningful backdoor equity (two diamonds on board, I hold none), so barreling as a bluff is thinly justified and will be called or raised by many hands in Flash’s calling range. Pot is 650; best play is to control the pot and concede to real strength. Plan: check and fold to any reasonably sized bet on the turn; if Flash checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (look to take a free card or find a feasible bluff spot only if the runout improves my equity or provides good blockers).

On the turn 3s, I have improved to bottom pair. The flop went check-check, which suggests Minni likely doesn't have a strong Ace or a Jack, although she could be slow-playing. My pair of threes is very weak and vulnerable, but it might be the best hand currently if Minni is holding overcards like KQ or KT. I will check to see if she decides to bet. If she bets, I will re-evaluate based on the size, but checking is the most standard play with a marginal hand out of position.

I have K6o and missed this board entirely. The Js Ad Jd 3s heavily favors Flash’s caller range (lots of Ax, Jx and pocket pairs) and I have no meaningful equity or strong blockers, so bluffing here is thin and likely to be called. With pot control paramount (pot = 650, deep stacks) I’ll check and take a free river — folding to any reasonably sized bet from Flash. If checked to on the river I’ll only consider a bluff if I pick up a King or another very favorable card that improves my blocker profile.

On the river 8s, the board is Js Ad Jd 3s 8s. I have a pair of threes. The action has been checked all the way through since the pre-flop raise. Minni's checks on the flop and turn suggest she either has a weak showdown value hand or has missed completely with overcards like KQ or QT. While my pair of threes is very weak, it does have some showdown value against her complete misses. Betting doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to fold out an Ace or a better pair, and I'd rather see a free showdown. I will check and hope she checks behind.

I missed the entire board with K6o on Js Ad Jd 3s 8s. Flash checked all streets, which is consistent with showdown-value hands (Ax, Jx, medium pairs) or a weak holding willing to take it to showdown — both of which are hard to fold. I have no spade and no meaningful blockers to Ax/Jx, so a river bluff has very poor fold equity against this caller-heavy line and with stacks this deep the risk outweighs the reward. Pot control is the correct line: take the free showdown rather than bloating the pot. I will check; if Flash bets a reasonable amount I will fold rather than chase a marginal bluff.

3s5c is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against calling ranges, and minimal straight potential that requires perfect conditions. In 3-handed play on the button, the opening range is wide (around 50-60%), but 35o ranks near the bottom and is not worth entering the pot voluntarily. Folding avoids difficult postflop spots OOP if raised, preserves stack for better opportunities, and maintains discipline over 150 hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h6s. The action has folded to me. While I could attempt to steal the Big Blind from Minni, 86 offsuit is a relatively weak hand to play out of position against a player who has shown some aggression in previous hands. Minni's stack is around 9200, making her somewhat dangerous to engage with marginal holdings. However, given it is only 50 chips more to call, and I want to maintain a balanced range, folding is the safest and most disciplined play to avoid a potentially difficult post-flop scenario with a weak hand.

I am on the button with 7c6c, a strong speculative hand suited-connector. In a three-handed game, this is a very profitable hand to play aggressively from the button. My current stack is healthy (146bb). I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds.

I’m in the small blind with Js4s facing a button open to 300. The pot is 450 and it costs me 250 more to call (300 total). Js4s is a marginal, often-dominated hand and I’ll be out of position postflop. The big blind still acts and can come along, making a multiway pot where my equity and implied odds are poor. A light 3‑bet bluff is possible but would risk more of my stack (and can be easily countered by a call/4‑bet from BB or BTN). On balance this is a fold — preserve chips and wait for a better, positionally favorable spot.

Preflop in BB facing a Button raise to 300 after SB folds. Pot 450, to call 200. Holding A6 suited diamonds, a strong speculative hand with ace high, suited for flush potential, good playability. Button ranges are wide in late position, often stealing blinds. Excellent pot odds (200 into 650 post-call, ~3:1), strong implied odds deep-stacked (15k+ effective). OOP but heads-up manageable. Folding too tight; 3-betting not ideal as A6s is thin for value/pressure vs wide range. Call to realize equity on favorable flops.

Preflop called a button steal raise with Ad6d suited for its playability, ace-high strength, and flush potential in position. Flop 2s5hKd is a miss: no pair, no strong draw (only backdoor diamond flush draw with Kd on board, ace-high no gutshot). Board is king-high and somewhat coordinated but dry (52 gaps miss most speculative hands). OOP as BB first to act, pot 650 deep-stacked (~15k effective). Button Flash has wide range including Kx (overpairs us), Ax, suited connectors, bluffs. C-betting here is thin for value (ace-high rarely best) and poor as bluff (range capped, fold equity low vs aggressive player who floats/calls wide). Checking optimal: realize equity by check-calling small cbets or check-raising value; pot control with marginal hand; induce bluffs from missed draws. Folding equity irrelevant OOP. Plan: check, fold to large bet, call small/shove draws if improves.

I raised pre-flop and the flop comes K-high and dry (2s 5h Kd). This board favors my range as the button raiser. Elon checked to me, which often signals weakness or a marginal hand. Even though I missed the board with 7c6c, this is a prime spot for a continuation bet to win the pot immediately. A small sizing (approx 45% of the pot) should be sufficient to fold out most of his non-pairing hands.

Flop checked as planned with ace-high no draw on Kd-high board, facing Flash's button c-bet of 300 into 650 (~46% pot). Hand has minimal showdown value (Ad6d blocks some Ax but Kd hits strong Kx in his wide button range heavily); equity too low (~15-20% vs polarized range of Kx/value/overpairs + thin bluffs/Ax) to justify calling for pot odds (300 into 1250 needs ~24%). No fold equity OOP. Folding realizes plan to fold to sizable cbets, preserves stack deep-stacked for better spots. Consistent with history where Flash cbets wide but values K-high boards.

Button with K♥Q♥ in a 3-handed cash game is a clear open-raise spot. There are only the blinds in (pot = 150) so calling 100 is weak passive play; raising takes the initiative and leverages position. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) — big enough to get folds from weaker holdings and keep SPR reasonable with my ~93bb stack, yet not so large that a standard 3-bet commits me unnecessarily. Plan: c-bet favorable flops and use position postflop; if faced with a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (call vs a standard 3-bet to see a flop in position, fold to too-large 4-bets).

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 300 (3x). My hand is KTo offsuit, which is marginal at best OOP. Button ranges are wide but often contain better kings like KQ/KJ and suited aces that dominate. Pot odds are decent (250 to call into 450 pot, needing ~35% equity), but postflop playability OOP is poor with this hand - many awkward spots on paired boards or uncoordinated flops. Deep stacks (150BB+), no need to defend this wide early in the session. Opponent history shows Minni aggressive but winning with raises; folding preserves stack for premium spots. BB may squeeze anyway.

I am in the big blind with K-3 offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. K-3 offsuit is generally a weak hand to defend against a 3x button open, even with the pot odds. Minni has been relatively active, and while I have a large stack, I should avoid calling with hands that are easily dominated or play poorly post-flop out of position. I'll fold this and wait for a better spot.

KQ suited on the button is a premium hand with excellent playability, high equity, and suited connectors potential for straights and flushes. Position is ideal for controlling the pot post-flop. Blinds are loose based on history (Minni raises frequently, Flash calls often), so raising standard 3x BB to 300 builds the pot, isolates weaker ranges, and takes initiative. Stacks are deep (150+ BB), so no need for larger size yet. Folding or limping is suboptimal.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h5s. Elon, the Button, has raised to 3x (300). This hand is too weak to defend, especially out of position in a three-handed game where ranges are wider but garbage hands like 7-5 offsuit still underperform significantly. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

Elon opened to 300 from the button in a three-handed pot. Pot is 450 and I must put in 200 more to call (300 total commitment). I hold Q♥6♥ — a suited queen with decent equity against a wide button open. Calling gives ~30.8% break-even equity (200 to win ~650), and Q6s has backdoor flush/straight potential and can realize decent equity vs a wide BTN range. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand by 3-betting; a small raise is likely to be called or 4-bet and would commit me OOP with a mediocre hand. Plan: flat-call, see the flop, play aggressively only with top pair/strong draws and otherwise control the pot or fold to heavy aggression. Commit total 300 to call.

Out of position on a dry, low flop (4c 3h 9c) with Q6♥. I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor heart draw (needs both turn+river, ~4%), though I do have ~24% to pair Q or 6 by the river. The button’s opening range is wide here and will c-bet often, but I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Check to control the pot, induce bluffs, and only continue cheaply — I’ll call small c-bets (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot ≈ 200–220) to realize equity and fold to larger-sized aggression. If I improve to a pair on the turn I will take a more aggressive line; otherwise preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Preflop raise with KQs suited from button is standard premium hand. Flop 4c 3h 9c is somewhat coordinated with two clubs and possible wheel straight draws, but I have King-high overcards blocking top pairs, backdoor spade flush draw, and great position heads-up vs Minni's BB calling range. She checked, indicating weakness. As preflop aggressor, continuation bet is standard to take initiative, deny equity to her draws/draw-heavy range, and extract value from weaker pairs/draws. Half-pot sizing (~325-400) balances value/protection without overcommitting deep stacks. History shows Minni calls loosely but folds to aggression often (e.g., Hand 16, 20). Checking risks giving free cards to improve her equity.

Fold. Elon barreled 400 into a 1050 pot — calling 400 to win 1450 requires ~27.6% equity. Q♥6♥ has no pair, only a weak backdoor heart (both turn+river hearts ~4.3%) and ~24% to pair by the river, so total realized equity is below break-even. I’m out of position and my pre-flop plan was to only continue to small c-bets (~≤1/3 pot); this 400 bet is larger than that threshold and is likely negative EV to call. Raising OOP as a bluff with this raggy holding is poor long-term strategy. Preserve stack for better, more justified spots — fold.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game. My hand, Jack-Five of spades, is a suited hand with some playability. In a 3-handed situation, opening ranges are significantly wider than in full-ring or 6-max games. A standard 3x big blind raise (300) from the button is a good way to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down immediately. If I am called, I will have the advantage of position throughout the rest of the hand. Given my recent history of opening for 300, this maintains a consistent image.

Facing a button open to 300 (pot 450) I would have to call 250 with 4♣6♠ from the small blind. This is a weak, unsuited, low-connectivity hand and I’ll be out of position postflop against both opponents. Calling requires ~36% equity just to break even on the immediate pot odds, which 4-6o doesn’t have versus a button open (and the BB can still call or 3-bet). A light 3-bet here would be a pure bluff and unnecessary given deep stacks and better spots to apply pressure. Fold and preserve my 9.1k stack for a stronger spot.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 after SB folds. Pot is 450, calling 200 more for total commitment of 300, offering about 2.25:1 odds. T9o is a decent speculative hand with straight potential and some high card value, suitable for defending wide against a button opening range which is likely broad (30-50% of hands based on Flash's aggressive history of frequent raises from late position). Deep stacks (15k effective) favor calling to realize equity postflop despite being OOP. Folding is too tight given pot odds and playability; 3-betting is unnecessary as it's not strong enough for value and risks bloating the pot OOP. Will call and proceed cautiously postflop, folding to aggression without improvement or floating for position if possible.

Flop is Ad 5h 3s, rainbow ace-high board, very dry. My T9o has no pair, no straight draw (gutshot would require 7-J, but no connectors here), no flush draw, just backdoor potential and a live ten. Equity is low (~20-30% vs. Flash's button c-bet range which includes all Ax, broadway, pairs, bluffs). OOP, first to act, pot 650. Best play is to check, realize no showdown value, and fold to any reasonable bet size. Donk betting is -EV with this weak hand as it invites raises and no fold equity. Flash is aggressive (frequent button raises/steals from history), likely c-bets 60-80% here, so check-fold maximizes EV by avoiding spew.

I have middle pair (5s) on an Ace-high board. Elon checked to me, which is standard for the Big Blind. While I have some showdown value, checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity. Betting might force out worse hands and only get called by better (like an Ace or a 5 with a better kicker). I'll check and see what the turn brings, keeping the pot small with a medium-strength hand.

Turn pairs the ace on a dry 3s5hAdAc board. My T9o has no pair, no draws, pure air with negligible showdown value. OOP first to act, pot 650. Checking flop induced a check from aggressive button Flash, possibly weakness or trap, but betting here with no equity or fold equity is -EV; it bloats pot and invites calls/raises from Ax, sets, etc. Best play is check-fold to any bet, preserving stack and avoiding spew. Flash likely bets value or turns hand into bluff, but we fold anyway. Prioritizing discipline over thin value attempts.

On the turn, the board has paired Aces (Ad, Ac). I have a pair of fives (5s) with a Jack kicker. Elon has checked twice now (flop and turn). My hand has decent showdown value against a range that might include smaller pairs or straight draws (like 4-2 or 6-4). Betting here doesn't achieve much; an Ace will never fold, and I'm likely ahead of most hands that would fold. By checking back, I control the pot size and can comfortably see a river. If Elon bets the river, I can evaluate based on the size, but checking keeps my marginal hand's value intact without risking a check-raise.

River 4h completes the 3-4-5 low end for wheel straights (A23456 but board has Aces, so 23456) and pairs the low cards slightly, but still a paired ace board with no flush. My T9o has zero equity left: no pair, no straight (T9 doesn't connect to 3-4-5), no flush draw, pure T-high bluff catcher at best, which loses to any Ax, pair, or straight. Preflop Flash button raised wide, I defended speculatively OOP, checked flop inducing check from presumed c-bet range, checked turn again on paired board. Flash's double check-back suggests possible weakness (missed draws, weak Ax like A2-A6, or total air turning into check-back), but betting here as donk OOP has terrible fold equity against calling stations/value hands, and no semi-bluff equity. Pot 650, deep stacks favor checking to showdown or folding to a bluff bet. Any bet I make gets called by better, spewing chips. Check preserves stack, realizes what little showdown value T-high has (unlikely best but free), and induces possible bluff from Flash's weak range. Fold equity irrelevant since I won't call a raise anyway. Disciplined check is +EV.

The board is 3s-5h-Ad-Ac-4h. I have a pair of fives (Js-5s). Elon has checked all three streets (flop, turn, and river). My hand has significant showdown value in this spot, especially since Elon has shown no aggression. A bet is unlikely to be called by a worse hand, and I don't want to risk a check-raise from a slow-played Ace or a straight (like 2-6 or 2-x). Checking back ensures I realize my equity and win the pot against most of Elon's range that missed the board.

Fold. 2♥8♥ is one of the weakest holdings — very little showdown value and frequently dominated. Although I'm on the button and could attempt a steal, both opponents are deep-stacked and have shown a tendency to defend and raise; calling 100 into a 150 pot would create a multiway spot where my equity is poor, and a raise (even as a bluff) risks facing a call or 3‑bet I can't profitably play. Preserve chips early in the session and wait for a better, more playable spot or a more favorable steal opportunity.

J♣2♠ is a trash hand: offsuit, disconnected, dominated by any Jx or better. In SB vs BB (Flash), calling 50 into 150 pot offers 3:1 odds (needing ~25% equity), and J2o has ~40% vs random but poor playability and reverse implied odds postflop. Flash plays aggressively (frequent raises from history), likely defends/raises weak calls. Folding preserves stack for better spots; limping weak hands leads to losses long-term in 150-hand game.

Ad3h is a marginal hand at best offsuit with poor connectivity and dominated by any other ace. In a 3-handed pot preflop on the button with deep stacks, but facing a 100 to call into a 150 pot (implying a raise or limp-raise situation), the pot odds are about 2.5:1 but equity realization is low postflop due to reverse implied odds if ace hits against better aces, and weak if misses. Opponents from history (Flash and Minni) are aggressive raisers but also fold often to 3-bets; however, calling or raising this trash risks spew over 150 hands. Position helps but not enough for this spot. Optimal play is to fold and wait for stronger holdings to maximize EV long-term.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. The action has folded to me. While A5o is not a premium hand, it is strong enough to raise for value and to attempt to win the blinds in a heads-up situation against Minni. Standard strategy in the Small Blind when folded to is to raise or fold; calling often allows the Big Blind to see a free flop or put us in a difficult position post-flop. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on Minni.

I have A♥K♦ in the big blind facing a 3bb open from the small blind (Flash). This is a premium hand heads-up and vs an SB open (likely a wide range) the correct play is to 3-bet for value and isolation. The pot is 400 and the call is 200, but a minimum reraise (to 500) is too small to achieve the fold equity and value I want. I’ll raise to 1200 total (~4x the open): large enough to force difficult decisions for weaker broadways and small pairs and to get value from Ax/Kx and many medium pairs, yet small enough relative to my 9000 stack to keep postflop maneuverability. If he folds I scoop the pot; if called I’ll play OOP with a plan to c-bet favorable A/K-high textures, control pot size on coordinated boards, and reevaluate versus any big 4-bet.

I raised to 300 from the Small Blind with Ace-Five offsuit, and Minni in the Big Blind has responded with a large 4x 3-bet to 1200. While I have an Ace blocker, A5o is quite weak against a 3-betting range, especially from an aggressive player like Minni who has shown strength in previous 3-bet pots (like Hand #4). Calling out of position with a hand that has poor playability is generally a losing strategy. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play to protect my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 5d6d, a strong suited connector that plays well in position. The current pot is small, and I am the first to act pre-flop. Raising here allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds or see a flop in position. Given the stack depths and my position, a standard 3x raise is appropriate.

Facing a 300 open from the button with Q♠3♣ in the small blind. This is a weak, frequently dominated offsuit queen with poor postflop playability and I’ll be out of position vs the opener (and the big blind still to act). Calling 250 to see a flop is cheap but will put me in tough spots against a wide but aggressive opening range; 3-betting to 500 as a bluff is higher variance and unnecessary with deep stacks. Prefer to preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold preflop.

3-handed preflop in BB facing a button min-raise to 300 (3x BB) after SB folds. 94s is a suited hand with playability: flush potential and straight draws possible. Pot odds are excellent (200 to call into 450 pot, ~31% pot odds). Vs button's wide opening range (~40-50% hands), 94s has solid equity (~40%+) heads-up and good implied odds deep-stacked. Defending wide OOP with speculative suited hands is standard. No need to 3-bet a marginal hand like this; fold is too tight given the price. Will call and play postflop OOP, looking to realize equity on favorable boards or bluff-catch.

Flop board is Tc Kd Kc, a paired kings board favoring Flash's button raising range heavily (many Kx, pocket pairs, Ax suited, etc.). My 94s is complete air: no pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), no straight draw (gutshot? No, 9-10 but board has Tc, no connection). Equity is very low (~10-20% vs typical button range, less vs value). OOP first to act in 650 pot, deep stacks, but no reason to bluff here – board is dryish but paired, hits opponents' range, and villain can easily call/raise with top pair/top kicker or better. Check-fold is optimal; realize I'm folding out cleaner hands but discipline > spew. Previous preflop call was fine for playability, but this board misses entirely.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on a paired board (Tc Kd Kc). While my hand (5d 6d) has missed completely, this board texture is generally better for my range as the button raiser than for Elon in the big blind. Elon's check is standard. I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot (300) to represent a King, a Ten, or a strong pocket pair, aiming to take the pot down immediately. If Elon calls, I can re-evaluate on the turn, but against many 'air' hands or small pocket pairs, this bet should find a fold.

Facing a 300 bet into a 950 pot on a Tc Kd Kc flop from the button after checking first OOP. 94s (4s9s) is pure air: no pair, no flush draw (spades vs. paired kings with two clubs), no straight draw. Flash's button raising range crushes this board (KK, Kx, ATs+, pairs, etc.), giving me <15% equity. Pot odds ~24% (300/1250) but reverse implied odds are terrible; calling commits to turn OOP with no hand. Bluffing unlikely to work as villain can call/raise profitably with his strong range hits. Standard check-fold; discipline preserves stack for better spots.

3♠4♣ (3h4s) is a very weak, non‑suited, low connector with little showdown value, but I’m on the button so I have postflop position against both blinds. It only costs 100 to see the flop into a 150 pot — a cheap price to realise equity and use position to fold or apply pressure when appropriate. Raising to try and “steal” is unnecessary with this hand because it will often be called by better ranges and bloat a pot I don’t want to play big. I’ll call 100 to see the flop; plan is to continue only with a real piece (pair, strong draw) or a favorable board texture, otherwise check‑fold or take a controlled bluff when appropriate. If I face a substantial 3‑bet preflop I’ll fold this holding.

Preflop in the small blind with AQo offsuit, a premium hand (top ~7-10% range). Button (Minni) limped, which is a weak range, often speculative hands. BB (Flash) yet to act. Standard play is to raise for value and to isolate the limper, building the pot with position advantage postflop while denying equity to weaker hands. Raising also charges dead money in the pot (250 already). Sizing to 4x the BB (400 total) is appropriate with deep stacks, larger than min-raise to fold out marginal calls. History shows opponents can fold to aggression (e.g., multiple folds to raises). This maximizes EV over many hands.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten-Trey offsuit (T3o), which is a very weak hand. Minni limped from the button and Elon raised to 400 from the Small Blind. Given the strength of the raise and the weakness of my hand, there is no reason to defend the blind or attempt a bluff in this multi-way scenario. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

3h4s is a very weak, offsuit hand — but I’m on the button, Flash folded and it’s cheap to see a flop in position. Elon raised to 400 after I had called 100, so I need to call 300 more. Calling 300 into a 600 pot gives me ~33% pot odds and the ability to use position postflop to fold when I miss or apply pressure selectively. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a bluff-raise that will be called or 3‑bet by stronger ranges. Plan: call to see the flop and continue only with a real piece (pair/strong draw) or a very favourable board; fold to significant preflop 3‑bet or heavy postflop aggression when I have no equity.

TPTK (top pair top kicker with AQ) on a highly coordinated flop with flush draw (all clubs) and straight draws (open-enders like 45, 58, 89). Minni limp-called pre-flop raise from button, indicating a wide range including suited connectors, Ax suited, broadway, pairs – many of which hit this board (sets, two-pair unlikely, draws likely). As OOP first to act, betting for thin value against weaker Qx/worse, protection vs. draws, and to deny equity/free cards. Cannot check-fold premium hand; check might induce bluffs but risks giving free cards to draws. Pot 900, bet 2/3 pot (600) balances value/protection while deep-stacked, charges draws, and history shows opponents fold to aggression often. Builds pot for our strong hand over 150-hand session.

Board is Qc 6c 7c — I hold 3h4s: no pair, no club, only four clean outs (the four 5s) to make a straight. The pot is 1500 and it costs 600 to call, so I need ≈28.6% equity to justify a call (600 / (1500+600)). My two‑street equity to hit a 5 by river is only ≈16.5%, well below the required pot‑odds. Elon opened preflop and is barreling this three‑club board, so his continuing range includes Qx, 6x/7x, club combos (including made two‑club flops or single‑club draws) and other strong hands that dominate me even if I hit. A raise as a bluff is unattractive — I have no club blockers and little fold equity against his likely range, and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a very weak hand. Per my preflop plan to only continue with a real piece or strong draw and to fold to significant post‑flop aggression when I have no equity, I’ll fold.

On the button with As6d facing an unopened pot (pot 150, to call 100 likely from blinds, SB probably folded providing dead money). A6s is a strong suited ace hand with good playability, straight/flush potential, blocks ace, dominates worse aces. Deep stacks (BB has ~88BB), excellent position. Standard to raise for value, build pot, and take initiative vs big blind who defends wide. Size to 300 (3x BB) balances value/protection. Folding or calling too passive.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 2h, which is a very weak starting hand. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here as calling out of position with trash hands leads to long-term losses, and there is no reason to attempt a 3-bet bluff against an unknown or wide range from the Button in this spot.

Elon opened to 300 from the button; pot is 450 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total). I have 8♥7♦ — a connected, playable hand with decent implied odds heads-up. Facing a standard 3bb open and with deep stacks (≈88bb) I prefer to defend rather than 3‑bet OOP with a marginal hand; a raise would commit me out of position with a hand that plays better seeing a flop. The call gives ~30% break‑even equity (200 to win ~650) and lets me realize equity postflop: I will proceed cautiously on dry, high boards (check/fold to heavy aggression) and play aggressively when I flop a pair, two pair, or a strong straight/straight draw. Call to see the flop.

Flop is 6c 7s 4d; I hold 8♥7♦ — top pair (sevens) with a decent 8 kicker and a 5-out gutshot to a straight. We're out of position vs the button who opened wide, so we want to both extract value from worse one-pair hands (6x,4x, weaker 7s) and charge the straight draw. Pot is ~650 — a half‑pot/controlled protection bet denies correct odds to draws while keeping the pot manageable OOP. Plan: bet ~350 to get value and charge draws; if he folds we take it, if he calls we re-evaluate on the turn (continue for value on safe cards, check/call or fold to big raises depending on sizing), and fold to a large raise that represents 8x/sets. Bet 350.

Preflop: Raised standard with A6s suited from button. Flop: 6c7s4d gives us pair of sixes (second pair) with Ace kicker, backdoor diamond flush draw. Decent but vulnerable hand with position. Pot 1000, villain (BB defender OOP) leads small 350 (~1/3 pot), which could be weak pairs, top pair weak kicker, draws (58,53,85 etc.), or monsters. Excellent pot odds (350 into 1000, ~3.8:1 direct + implied). Calling maintains pot control, realizes equity, keeps position for turn. Folding too tight preflop call; raising thin value/bluff risks stack (her 8300) when we can realize equity cheaper. Plan: Call, evaluate turn aggression based on board/runout.

Check. I bet the flop for value with top pair (7s) and got called, but the Ah on the turn is a dangerous card — it heavily improves the button’s preflop calling/raising range (many Ax, plus A6/A4 two‑pair combinations). Out of position with only thin value and no flush draws to fear, I prefer pot control: betting risks a large raise that would force me off my hand, while checking lets me call small bets to realize equity and induce bluffs. Plan: check and evaluate Elon's action — call small-to-medium turn bets (≈≤½ pot ≈ ≤675) to realize my ~28% to improve / extract value from worse hands, fold to large pot-sized overbets (>≈⅔–full pot) that strongly represent an Ace/two‑pair, and if he checks back re-evaluate the river (bet small for value on safe cards, check behind on scary runouts).

Turn card Ah pairs the top card on the board, improving us to top two pair (Aces and Sixes) on a 6c7s4dAh board. This is a very strong hand, near nuts except vs. sets (unlikely), 58 straight, or unlikely higher two pair. Villain led small on flop (pair/draw range), we called with second pair + overcard, now she checks OOP after pairing top card. Her check suggests weakness: missed ace/draw, medium strength afraid to bloat pot, or trapping (less likely given small flop lead). Pot 1350, her stack 8300, our position excellent. Value bet to charge draws (5x, flush draws unlikely), worse pairs (77,44,76 etc.), weaker Ax. Bet sizing ~2/3 pot (900) extracts value from calling range while denying equity. Checking induces bluffs/call-downs but misses value; raising irrelevant (no bet). Plan: bet 900, call or shove river likely.

I call 900. I have top pair (7s) with an 8-kicker — a decent but vulnerable hand OOP after an A on the turn. Elon's 900 into a 2250 pot is a small/medium sizing (~40% pot) that can be both value and a bluff. Pot math: calling 900 to win roughly 4050 requires ~22% equity, which my hand usually has vs a BTN open/call-on-flop range (he will have many worse pairs, missed draws and bluffs, plus some Ax/sets). Raising OOP to 1800 would bloat the pot and is likely to be punished by Aces/sets; folding is too weak given the pot odds and my hand. Plan: call and evaluate the river — fold to very large overbets if I don’t improve, call reasonable sized river bets, and consider a small value bet on safe rivers or check-back to control the pot against strong ranges.

River pairs the Ace — my hand is now two pair (Aces & Sevens) with an 8 kicker. Elon’s line (preflop open, call of my flop bet, then a sizeable turn raise into my check) is weighted toward strong holdings: Ax and flop-sets/7x are all plausible. On this river those combos convert to trips or full houses and will beat my two‑pair. Betting into that range only bloats a pot where I’m often behind and will mostly get called (or raised) by better hands; a small bet may fold some air but won’t change that fact. I’ll control the pot and take showdown: check. Plan post‑river: call a reasonable value bet (small/half‑pot) from bluffs/weaker two‑pairs, but fold to large polarized overbets that credibly represent full houses/quads.

River Ac pairs the Aces on the board, giving me AAA with a 6 kicker (trips Aces), an extremely strong hand that crushes villain's likely range. Board: 6c7s4dAhAc is paired, villain defended BB wide preflop, led small flop (weak pairs/draws like 76,54,53,85, A-high, small pairs), checked turn after Ah (missed or weak), called my 900 turn value bet, now checks river again OOP. Her range: weaker Ax (Ax7, Ax6, Ax4, KQ etc.), two-pair (76,74,64), stubborn pairs (77,44), unlikely sets/straights given action. No flush. Very few hands beat us: A6 specifically (unlikely), 66/77/44 full houses improbable. Pot 3150, villain 7400 effective, position advantage. Massive value bet to extract from Ax, two-pair, overpairs. Size 2500 (~3/4 pot) gets calls from worse while leaving her room to call (calls into 5650 pot with good odds but her stack ~4900 behind). Smaller risks cheap showdown; larger risks fold. Checking misses huge value. Plan: Bet 2500, expect call or fold, shove any raise.

Board is 6c 7s 4d Ah Ac and I hold 8h7d — two pair, Aces & Sevens. Elon’s line (button open, call on flop, a large turn raise that I called, and now a ~2.5k river into a 5.65k pot ≈44% pot) is weighted toward Ax and strong made hands (trips/boats), but also can include missed draws and worse one-pair hands. Calling 2.5k to win ~8.15k gives ~30.7% pot‑odds; my two‑pair has roughly that equity vs his likely river range and will beat bluffs and many weaker pairs. Raising here is thin and just bloats the pot when I’m often behind (an Ace or a full house crushes me) and could induce a shove. Folding gives up too much equity. I will call to reach showdown and avoid committing more out of position.

I am on the button with J3o. While it is a marginal hand, I could potentially steal the blinds with a raise. However, Minni in the Small Blind and Elon in the Big Blind have both been active and aggressive in recent hands. J3o is too weak to defend against a 3-bet or to play post-flop out of position if the small blind calls and the big blind stays in. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I’m on the button (small blind) heads-up against the big blind with T5o and ~48.5BB effective. This is a clear open-raise spot: position post-flop is a big advantage and I want to seize the initiative and generate fold equity. A standard 3x BB (raise to 300 total) balances my range, puts pressure on his wide defending range, and keeps the pot a manageable size if he calls. If he 3-bets large I’ll fold this marginal hand; if he calls I’ll play post-flop in position and c-bet / realize equity as appropriate. Calling the extra 50 would be too passive and min-raising to 200 is too small against a sticky big blind, so I raise to 300.

95o is a complete trash hand with poor playability, no straight or flush potential that connects well. Facing a standard raise from SB, who has a wider range but still stronger than mine. OOP postflop would be disastrous. Pot odds are 200 to call into 400 (3:1), but hand equity and playability too low. Minni's stack 4600 allows for continued aggression if I call. Standard fold to preserve chips for better spots.

Button vs two blinds, 3-handed with Ah6d. This is a profitable open from the button — I want to take the initiative and play the hand in position. I’ll open to a standard ~3x BB to 300 (total): big enough to charge the blinds and deter light cold-calls/steals, but small relative to my 50bb stack so I retain post-flop maneuverability. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet/shove that closes action, call small-to-medium 3-bets and play postflop in position, c-betting favorable runouts for value and fold equity.

A7o is a marginal offsuit ace from the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (3x). Pot odds are favorable (calling 250 into 450, ~35% equity needed), and it has decent equity against a wide button opening range (~40-45%). However, post-flop position is terrible OOP against the button, with poor playability, reverse implied odds if dominated by better ace, and potential multiway pot with BB. Opponents like Minni have shown aggression, increasing risk of strong hands or shoves. Deep stacks (mine 199BB, Minni 47BB) allow waiting for better spots without forcing marginal defense. Folding preserves stack for premium opportunities over 150 hands.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc 5d. Minni, who is on the button and has a relatively short stack of 47 big blinds (4700), has raised to 300. To call, I need to put in 200 more into a pot that will be 650. While the pot odds (3.25 to 1) are somewhat attractive, J5 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Against a button open from a player who has shown aggression in previous hands, folding is the standard and most disciplined play to avoid losing more chips in a marginal situation.

Tc3h is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against any calling or 3-betting range. On the button in a 3-handed pot, acting first preflop, folding preserves my deep stack (nearly 200BB) for better opportunities. Stealing blinds with this hand risks too much for minimal gain against potentially wide defending ranges, especially with Minni's shorter stack in the BB. Standard discipline: fold trash preflop.

I am in the Small Blind with a pocket pair of Fours (4c 4s). Pocket pairs are always worth playing in a three-handed game, especially when folded to the blinds. I have the option to complete the small blind for 50 more or raise. Against Minni in the Big Blind, who has a relatively short stack (50.5 big blinds), I want to take control of the pot but also avoid getting into a massive pre-flop confrontation where I'm likely flipping or behind. A standard raise to 300 (3x) is a solid play here to either win the blinds immediately or set up a post-flop situation where I have the initiative.

Facing a 300 open from the small blind as the big blind with 4h7h. This is a weak but suited hand with backdoor flush/straight potential; I already have 100 in and need 200 more to call. The pot (400) gives me roughly 3:1 pot odds to see a flop and Flash’s SB open to 300 is likely a wide range, so defending is correct. I’m out of position postflop and don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3-bet bluff, so I’ll call and see a flop; fold to heavy aggression if I miss, continue with draws or any pair/strong combos.

On a board of As-7c-8d, as the pre-flop aggressor, I have a significant range advantage. This board contains an Ace, which is a card much more likely to be in my raising range from the Small Blind than in Minni's calling range from the Big Blind. Even though my pocket Fours are weak here, a continuation bet (C-bet) is standard strategy to represent a strong Ace and force a fold from her air or weak holdings. I'll bet 300 into the 600 pot (50% pot) to maintain initiative.

Flop: A♠ 7♣ 8♦ — I hold 7♥4♥: bottom pair with a very weak kicker and backdoor hearts. Flash opened preflop from the SB (wide range) and c‑bet small (300 into a 900 pot), which is a highly frequency/merge c‑bet rather than a polar value shove. Calling 300 is the correct play: the pot odds (~4:1) demand only ~20% equity and my pair is ahead of a lot of missed hands and bluffs in his range. I’m in position, so I can control the pot on the turn and fold to clear strength (big turn bet or obvious two‑pair+/Ace/8). Raising here would bloat the pot with a vulnerable hand and will often get me into uncomfortable spots against Ax/8x; folding surrenders too much equity for a small cost. Plan: call now, re-evaluate on the turn — proceed for value if I improve, or fold to heavy aggression on scary cards.

The turn card is the Seven of Diamonds, which pairs the board. My pocket Fours have very little showdown value now, as any Ace, Seven, or Eight in Minni's range beats me. Since Minni called the flop continuation bet on an Ace-high board, it is highly likely she has a piece of the board (most likely a weak Ace or an Eight) or a straight draw (like 9-10 or 5-6). By checking, I am looking to control the size of the pot and potentially see a cheap river. If she bets, I will likely have to fold as my hand hasn't improved and she has shown interest in the pot.

I have 7♥4♥ on A♠ 7♣ 8♦ 7♦ — that’s trips (7s) on the turn. Flash opened wide from the SB and c-bet the flop, then checked the turn, so his range is wide: Ax, 8x, some medium pairs, draws and bluffs. My trips are strong but can be behind sets/boats (AA, 88, or A7), so I don’t want to just check and give a free river. Betting for value and protection is best. A half‑pot bet (~600 into 1200) is large enough to charge draws and extract value from worse Ax and 8x and calling floats, but small enough to keep some of those hands in to be paid off. Plan: bet 600 on turn, re-evaluate on the river — if Flash raises significantly I’ll give strong consideration to folding (he’s increasingly likely to have set/full house), but if he calls I’ll size my river action based on the card and his tendencies.

On the turn, the Seven of Diamonds pairs the board. My pocket Fours are now significantly weakened. Minni called a pre-flop raise and a continuation bet on an Ace-high flop, which strongly suggests she has a piece of the board (likely an Ace, an Eight, or a Seven). While her bet of 600 into 1200 could be a probe bet after I checked, my hand has almost no showdown value and very few outs to improve. If she has an Eight, she now has a better two-pair (Eights and Sevens) than my Fours and Sevens. If she has an Ace or a Seven, I am drawing nearly dead. Given her stack size and the likelihood that her flop-calling range dominates my small pocket pair, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 7h 5c. This is a very weak hand, and while it can sometimes be played in a heads-up or late-position situation, I am facing both Minni (who has been active) and Elon (who has a large stack). Given my current stack and the hand's low equity, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

6♣2♠ is one of the weakest hands in poker — unsuited, unconnected, and almost always dominated. I’m out of position (SB vs BB) and have already posted 50. To call 50 only gets 25% pot odds but the hand has terrible playability and very poor implied odds against a typical BB defending range. Raising as a blind-steal bluff is an option, but Elon (BB) is deep and tends to defend/call; that would put me in difficult postflop spots with a hand that rarely improves. Long-term EV is best by folding and waiting for a better spot, so I’ll muck and conserve chips for better opportunities.

Fold equity + position. On the button in a 3-way spot with Q6o and ~57BB effective, this is a standard steal opportunity — Q6o is marginal but fine to open from the button. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) to apply pressure, avoid giving callers great pot odds, and try to get heads-up or take the pot down preflop. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet (hand is too weak to continue vs strong ranges), call or proceed postflop if I get single or multi-way calls and use position to control the pot and extract value on favorable boards.

Preflop in the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (3x BB). My hand J♥2♥ is suited but extremely weak with poor connectivity and dominated by any better J or suited hand Minni might have. J2s has low equity against a typical button opening range (~40-50% hands), and postflop playability is marginal at best. Pot odds are about 1.8:1 (250 to call for 700 pot after), but this hand doesn't warrant a defend here, especially 3-handed where button is wide but SB defends tighter. Minni has been aggressive in history but folding trash preserves stack for better spots. No fold equity squeeze viable with dead BB money low.

I am in the Big Blind with T5s, which is a marginal suited connector. Minni has raised from the Button to 300, requiring me to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650. Given the pot odds (3.25:1) and being suited, it is often a defend in a heads-up situation, but I am out of position. However, looking at the stack sizes, Minni has a relatively short stack (5400) compared to me and Elon. I've seen Minni be aggressive in previous hands. T5s has decent playability on many flops, so I will call to see a flop and utilize my position if the board hits my range or offers a bluffing opportunity.

On a board of Jc 9s 2d, I have Ten-high with a backdoor spade draw. As the Big Blind checking to the pre-flop raiser (Minni) is the standard and most prudent play. I have no reason to lead out here as this board favors the Button's opening range more than my defensive range, and I want to see how she reacts to the flop before committing more chips with a very weak hand.

I opened from the button and got heads-up to this relatively dry J♣ 9♠ 2♦ flop. I have Q♦6♠ — a high overcard with a little showdown value and decent fold equity as the preflop aggressor. Flash defended a wide range from the BB, so a continuation bet will fold many weak hands and charge any draws while keeping the pot controllable. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot to apply pressure but preserve stack depth; plan is to fold to a large raise (Q6o has poor equity vs strong action), call small resistance and proceed cautiously on the turn, or check-back if I’m called and a scary card comes.

On a board of Jc 9s 2d, my Ten-high and backdoor flush draw are extremely weak. While I am getting decent pot odds (over 3:1) to call, I am out of position against a player who has shown aggression. Floating here with virtually no equity or a clear path to winning the hand is unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Pocket Jacks is a premium hand, top of my 3-handed opening range from the button. Raising preflop builds the pot for value, takes initiative, and leverages my position postflop. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate given the blinds and observed opening sizes in prior hands. Limping risks a multiway pot without initiative; folding is -EV with this strength. Opponents have shown aggression but JJ crushes wide calling ranges.

I am in the Small Blind with 4c 2h, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. Given the extremely poor playability and lack of equity of my hand, there is no reason to call or defend, even against a wide Button opening range. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
